data.title: Entegris, Inc. Just Beat Analyst Forecasts, And Analysts Have Been Updating Their Predictions
data.description:
Entegris, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENTG) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 8.8% to US$142 in the week after its latest quarterly results. Revenues were US$812m, approximately in line with whatthe analysts expected, although statutory earnings per share (EPS) crushed expectations, coming in at US$0.60, an impressive 32% ahead of estimates. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. So we gathered the latest post-earnings forecasts to see what estimates suggest is in store for next year.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Following the latest results, Entegris' ten analysts are now forecasting revenues of US$3.44b in 2026. This would be a reasonable 6.4% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to jump 48% to US$2.58. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of US$3.42b and earnings per share (EPS) of US$2.28 in 2026. There was no real change to the revenue estimates, but the analysts do seem more bullish on earnings, given the solid gain to earnings per share expectations following these results.

See our latest analysis for Entegris

The analysts have been lifting their price targets on the back of the earnings upgrade, with the consensus price target rising 8.7% to US$158. It could also be instructive to look at the range of analyst estimates, to evaluate how different the outlier opinions are from the mean. Currently, the most bullish analyst values Entegris at US$205 per share, while the most bearish prices it at US$115. This shows there is still a bit of diversity in estimates, but analysts don't appear to be totally split on the stock as though it might be a success or failure situation.

One way to get more context on these forecasts is to look at how they compare to both past performance, and how other companies in the same industry are performing. The period to the end of 2026 brings more of the same, according to the analysts, with revenue forecast to display 8.6% growth on an annualised basis. That is in line with its 8.0% annual growth over the past five years. Compare this with the broader industry (in aggregate), which analyst estimates suggest will see revenues grow 21% annually. So it's pretty clear that Entegris is expected to grow slower than similar companies in the same industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest takeaway for us is the consensus earnings per share upgrade, which suggests a clear improvement in sentiment around Entegris' earnings potential next year. On the plus side, there were no major changes to revenue estimates; although forecasts imply they will perform worse than the wider industry. We note an upgrade to the price target, suggesting that the analysts believes the intrinsic value of the business is likely to improve over time.

With that said, the long-term trajectory of the company's earnings is a lot more important than next year. At Simply Wall St, we have a full range of analyst estimates for Entegris going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here..

data.title: Western Digital (WDC) One Off Gain Lifts Margins And Tests Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Western Digital (WDC) has put up another big quarter, with Q2 2026 revenue at US$3.0b and basic EPS of US$5.27, backed by trailing twelve month revenue of US$10.7b and EPS of US$11.40 that reflect a very large year over year earnings swing. Over recent quarters, the company has seen revenue move from US$2.2b in Q1 2025 to US$3.0b in Q2 2026, while basic EPS has shifted from US$0.43 to US$5.27. This sets up a story where higher reported profitability, supported by a 36.8% trailing net margin that includes a sizeable one off gain, is front and center for investors weighing the quality and durability of these results.

See our full analysis for Western Digital.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the key bullish and bearish narratives investors follow around Western Digital, and where the latest results start to challenge those views.

See what the community is saying about Western Digital

Advertisement

Margins Inflated by One Off Gain

  • Trailing net margin is 36.8% versus 4.4% a year ago, and includes a US$984.0m one off gain that has a major impact on the reported profitability picture.
  • Bulls talk about long term margin expansion as AI driven storage demand grows, yet this margin jump leans heavily on a non recurring gain, which means:
    • The bullish view of structurally stronger net margins sits alongside earnings that are temporarily lifted by the US$984.0m item.
    • Forecast earnings growth of about 8.6% per year is much lower than the very large trailing earnings swing, so the bullish case rests more on future demand than on the current 36.8% margin.
Over the last year, bulls argue Western Digital’s margin story is just getting started. However, this result is heavily shaped by that US$984.0m boost, so it is worth reading their full argument in context before leaning on it too much 🐂 Western Digital Bull Case.

Revenue Growth Versus Earnings Forecasts

  • Revenue is forecast to grow about 19.5% per year, while earnings are forecast to grow about 8.6% per year, so sales expectations sit well ahead of profit growth in the current models.
  • Consensus narrative points to strong demand from hyperscalers and AI related storage, yet the lower earnings growth forecast compared with revenue suggests:
    • Analysts see room for higher volumes, but also expect margins to move down from the current 36.8% level toward lower long term levels.
    • The gap between revenue and earnings growth means higher sales alone may not automatically translate into equally strong profit growth if costs or pricing offset part of the top line gains.

Premium P/E With Big DCF Gap

  • Western Digital trades on a P/E of 37.1x compared with a Global Tech average of 23.8x and peer average of 29.4x, while the share price of US$431.52 sits well below a DCF fair value of about US$1,059.39 in this dataset.
  • Bears highlight the rich P/E as a key concern, but the valuation data also shows a very large gap to DCF fair value, which means:
    • The bearish view that the stock is expensive on earnings multiples is supported by the 37.1x P/E premium to industry and peers.
    • At the same time, a DCF fair value more than double the current price challenges the idea that the valuation is stretched on every measure, and shows how sensitive conclusions are to the cash flow assumptions behind each model.
Skeptics focus on that 37.1x P/E, but the wide spread to DCF fair value gives plenty for the cautious camp to unpack further 🐻 Western Digital Bear Case

Approach 1: FactSet Research Systems Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a company can create above the return that shareholders require. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it compares the profit generated on each dollar of equity with the cost of that equity.

For FactSet Research Systems, book value is estimated at $58.11 per share, with a stable book value of $68.67 per share based on weighted future estimates from 4 analysts. Stable EPS is $20.23 per share, also sourced from those forward Return on Equity estimates. Against a cost of equity of $5.63 per share, this produces an excess return of $14.60 per share, supported by an average Return on Equity of 29.47%.

Using this profile, the Excess Returns model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $373.69 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $227.58, this implies a 39.1% discount, which screens as meaningfully undervalued on this method.

For established, profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay today directly to the earnings the business is already generating. It lets you compare how the market prices each dollar of profit across different stocks.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings appear. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower multiple.

FactSet Research Systems currently trades on a P/E of 14.11x. That sits below the Capital Markets industry average P/E of 41.87x and the peer group average of 24.16x. data.title: NovoCure’s Optune Pax Win And TRIDENT Data Shape Growth Outlook
data.description:
• NovoCure (NasdaqGS:NVCR) received FDA approval for Optune Pax for locally advanced pancreatic cancer and has started its U.S. commercial launch.
  • The new therapy has seen early adoption and payer coverage in the U.S., expanding NovoCure's reach beyond its original focus in glioblastoma.
  • Pivotal Phase 3 data from the TRIDENT trial in newly diagnosed glioblastoma are expected later this year.

NovoCure focuses on tumor treating fields therapy, a medical technology designed to disrupt cancer cell division using electric fields. With Optune Pax now approved for pancreatic cancer in the U.S., the company is moving beyond its existing presence in glioblastoma into an area of high unmet need. For investors, this places attention on how NovoCure executes the launch and builds patient and physician uptake around this new indication.

The upcoming TRIDENT Phase 3 readout in newly diagnosed glioblastoma is another key event for NasdaqGS:NVCR to watch. Together, commercial traction for Optune Pax and the glioblastoma data could influence how investors view the breadth of the tumor treating fields platform, although the ultimate impact will depend on detailed clinical and adoption trends that are still to come.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for NovoCure by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on NovoCure.

2 things going right for NovoCure that this headline doesn't cover.

The FDA approval and U.S. launch of Optune Pax fit directly into NovoCure’s existing earnings story, which currently combines growing sales with sizeable losses. In Q1 2026, net revenue of US$174.06 million and a net loss of US$71.14 million underline how dependent the company still is on scaling its tumor treating fields platform. Optune Pax for locally advanced pancreatic cancer adds a new revenue stream on top of Optune Gio in glioblastoma and Optune Lua in lung cancer, so uptake here matters for how quickly fixed costs are spread across a broader patient base.

Early Optune Pax metrics, including 868 certified prescribers, 169 prescriptions in the first seven weeks, and initial coverage from Elevance Health, show that NovoCure is starting to extend beyond its original glioblastoma franchise. For you as an investor, the focus is less on any single quarter and more on whether this launch, alongside future lung and other indications, helps NovoCure move closer to its full year 2026 revenue guidance range of US$690 million to US$710 million. The upcoming Phase 3 TRIDENT data in newly diagnosed glioblastoma are also important, because positive results could reinforce the commercial story for the core brain cancer indication that still drives much of the business today.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The NovoCure Narrative

  • The Optune Pax approval and launch align with the narrative that expanded indications and broader reimbursement can support access, higher utilization, and more stable long term revenue from tumor treating fields therapy.
  • At the same time, ongoing net losses and the need for additional commercial spending to support Optune Pax, Optune Lua, and future launches show that profitability challenges raised in the narrative remain in place.
  • The specific early data points from the pancreatic launch, such as the number of certified prescribers and rapid payer coverage from a large U.S. insurer, are not fully reflected in the narrative and may influence how investors view adoption and reimbursement risk.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for NovoCure to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ NovoCure remains loss making, with a Q1 2026 net loss of US$71.14 million and basic loss per share of US$0.62, so the path to sustainable profitability is still uncertain.
  • ⚠️ Heavy reliance on tumor treating fields technology across a limited product portfolio means any clinical setbacks, regulatory issues, or competitive therapies from larger oncology players such as Medtronic, Varian (Siemens Healthineers), or Roche could weigh heavily on future revenue.
  • 🎁 The Optune Pax approval in pancreatic cancer, combined with existing Optune Gio and Optune Lua franchises, broadens NovoCure’s exposure across multiple solid tumor indications and geographies, creating more ways to support revenue growth.
  • 🎁 Improved 2026 net revenue guidance of US$690 million to US$710 million and management’s focus on new indication launches suggest that, if adoption keeps building, the company could potentially scale its fixed cost base over a larger revenue pool.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on several moving parts. For Optune Pax, watch prescription trends, the mix of academic versus community prescribers, and how quickly additional U.S. payers follow Elevance Health in publishing coverage policies. For glioblastoma, the Phase 3 TRIDENT readout in newly diagnosed patients is central to how physicians view tumor treating fields in front line care and could influence use of Optune Gio. It is also worth tracking how Optune Lua in lung cancer contributes to active patient growth alongside pancreatic and brain cancer indications, as that will indicate whether NovoCure’s platform is gaining traction across multiple tumor types.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are asking whether Enterprise Products Partners is still reasonably priced after a long run in the unit price, this article walks through what the current valuation really reflects and where the key pressure points are.
  • The units last closed at US$38.03, with stated returns of 0.1% over 7 days, 1.6% over 30 days, 18.3% year to date, 33.3% over 1 year, 83.1% over 3 years and 134.3% over 5 years, which gives plenty of context for a closer look at value.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Enterprise Products Partners in the context of broader interest in energy infrastructure and income focused investments, which has kept attention on its distribution profile and asset base. This backdrop helps explain why valuation has become a key talking point as investors weigh income, risk and price.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Enterprise Products Partners scores 5 out of 6, so the next sections compare what different valuation methods suggest about the current price and then finish with a way to tie those numbers to the broader investment story.

Find out why Enterprise Products Partners's 33.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Enterprise Products Partners Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Enterprise Products Partners, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach built on cash flow projections. The latest twelve months Free Cash Flow is about $4.18b, and analysts plus extrapolations point to projected Free Cash Flow of about $8.14b in 2030, with a path of forecast and estimated cash flows in between. Simply Wall St uses analyst estimates where available, then extends the trend for the outer years.

On this basis, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $92.83 per unit, compared with the recent unit price of $38.03. That gap implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 59.0%, indicating that, based purely on these cash flow assumptions, the units appear materially undervalued on this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Enterprise Products Partners is undervalued by 59.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Enterprise Products Partners.

Approach 2: Enterprise Products Partners Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business that is regularly generating earnings, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it connects what you pay per unit today to the earnings that are already in place.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Enterprise Products Partners currently trades on a P/E of 14.1x. That sits close to the Oil and Gas industry average of about 14.6x, and below the broader peer group average of 21.4x. This gives a rough sense of how the market is pricing its earnings relative to others.

Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” goes further and estimates what P/E might be reasonable given Enterprise Products Partners earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors. Because it adjusts for these company specific features, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with industry and peer averages.

For Enterprise Products Partners, the Fair Ratio stands at 27.7x, which is higher than the current 14.1x. On this earnings based yardstick, the units screen as undervalued.
